'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-qqth centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-qqth centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buqqhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-qqth centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buqqhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-qqth centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-qqth centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}

[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}

Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}

* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ... == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. '''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.
* {{ill|Jōnangū|ja|城南宮}} – Starting point from Kyoto * {{ill|Hakkenya Dock|ja|八軒家船着場}} – Landing dock from Kyoto to Osaka
1. {{ill|Kubotsu Ōji|ja|窪津王子}} 2. {{ill|Sakaguchi Ōji|ja|坂口王子}} ...
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ... == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}

After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=draft}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子|12=draft}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|12=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=draft}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野|12=draft}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=draft}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=draft}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=draft}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=draft}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社|12=draft}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=draft}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=draft}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=draft}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=draft}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=draft}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子|12=draft}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|12=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.

The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>

The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=draft}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子|12=draft}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|12=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=draft}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野|12=draft}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=draft}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=draft}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=draft}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=draft}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社|12=draft}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=draft}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=draft}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=draft}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=draft}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=draft}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子|12=draft}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|12=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} <!--Kujūku Ōji--> <!--Kujūku Ōji-->
<!--Kujūku Ōji-->
<!--Kujūku Ōji--> {{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}
]]

The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|12=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=draft}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野|12=draft}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=draft}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=draft}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=draft}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=draft}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社|12=draft}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=draft}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=draft}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=draft}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=draft}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=draft}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子|12=draft}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|12=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|12=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|12=draft}} <!--Kujūku Ōji--> <!--Kujūku Ōji-->

The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}
{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} {{draft categories|
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Shugendō Deities]] [[Category:Shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }}
[[ja:九十九王子]] [[en:Kujūku Ōji]] [[sn:Kujūku Ōji]] [[fr:Kujūkuōji]] [[sn:ujūku Ōji]]
<!--Kujūku Ōji--> <!--Kujūku Ōji--> <!--Kujūku Ōji--> <!--Kujūku Ōji-->

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道}}
{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} {{draft categories|
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Shugendō Deities]] [[Category:Shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kujūku Ōji]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qq]][[ja:Kujūkuōji]] }}
[[ja:九十九王子]] [[en:Kujūku Ōji]] [[sn:Kujūku Ōji]] [[fr:Kujūku Ōji]] [[sn:ujūku Ōji]]

revision.comment: 84 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:九十九王子 up to rev 102494402
revision.text:
'''九十九王子'''（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた[[修験道|熊野修験]]の手で急速に組織された一群の神社のことをいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。したがって、その分布は[[紀伊路]]・[[中辺路]]の沿道に限られる。 [[ファイル:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|多富気王子跡（[[大門坂]]）]]
== 概要 == === 出現と盛衰 === 王子の名の初出史料と考えられているのは、[[増基]]の手による参詣記『いほぬし（庵主）』にある「わうじのいはや（王子の岩屋）」で、[[花窟神社|花の窟]]についての記事に登場する。この文書の成立年代は[[10世紀]]後半から[[11世紀]]半ばまでと見られているが、これに次ぐのは[[1081年|1082]]（[[永保|永保元]]）年の[[藤原為房]]の日記『[[大御記]]』の日根王子での[[奉幣]]の記事であり、少なくとも[[11世紀]]には熊野に王子と呼ばれる神社があったことが確認できる。[[12世紀]]に入ると王子社が急増乱立し、史料にも新王子の記述が増える。[[13世紀]]になってもしばらくはこの傾向が続き、12 - 13世紀に最盛期を迎える。だが、こうした新王子の中には、短命のものもかなりあった。その後、おおむね[[鎌倉時代]]以降に、熊野詣の主体の変化や熊野詣自体の後退に伴って、多くは衰退するにいたった。
=== 王子とは何か === 王子は参詣途上で儀礼を行う場所であった。主たる儀礼は奉幣と経供養（[[般若心経]]などを読経する）であり、神仏混淆的である。だが、よく言われるような[[熊野三山]]遥拝が行われた形跡は（少なくとも史料上では）確認できない。また、帰路にはほとんど顧みられることがないことから、物品の補給をおこなったとする説もあたらないと考えられている。これらの儀式が王子で行われたのは、王子とは[[熊野権現]]の御子神であるとの認識があり、すなわち参詣者の庇護が期待されたのである。
これら王子の形成は、中世熊野詣において先達をつとめた熊野の修験者によるものである。本来は沿道住人の祀る雑多な在地の神々である諸社を王子と認定したのは、熊野参詣を主導する先達たちである（ただし解釈は一貫せず、参詣記ごと・先達ごとのばらつきが相当ある）。また、'''王子'''という命名も、峯中修行者を守護する神仏は童子の姿をとるという[[修験道]]の思想に基づくものであると考えられる。同時に、熊野修験は院政期以降の皇族・貴人たちの参詣の先達をつとめたが、このことは、九十九王子の顕著な特徴である、紀伊路・中辺路への集中や院政期に重なる12世紀の大量出現をもたらしている。実際、最盛期に80余を数えた王子の大半は、皇族の参詣の活発化に伴って組織されたものである。
=== 五体王子 === [[ファイル:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|藤代王子]] [[ファイル:切目王子.jpg|thumb|切目王子]] [[ファイル:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|滝尻王子]] [[ファイル:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|発心門王子]] 九十九王子の中には五体王子（ごたいおうじ）と呼ばれる王子があり、他とは格式を異にするとされる。これらは一般に、熊野の主神の御子神ないし眷属神として三山に祀られる神々のなかでも、五所王子と呼ばれる神々（若一王子・禅師宮・聖宮・児宮・子守宮）を祀る神社であり、三山から勧請したものと考えられている。これら五体王子では、舞・[[白拍子]]・[[神楽]]・[[猿楽]]などが行われており、芸能的性格が強いと一応は言えるが、これらは他の王子でも行われており、顕著な特徴とは言えない。なお、五'''大'''王子と書くのは誤りである。
いずれの王子を五体王子に数えるかは解釈の相違があり、[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記では[[籾井（樫井）王子|籾井（樫井）]]・[[藤代王子|藤代]]・[[稲葉根王子|稲葉根]]の3社を挙げるが、[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽院]]参詣記は[[藤代王子]]のみが該当し、[[稲葉根王子]]がこれに準じるとしているが、他の列挙例もあって一貫しない。現在では、[[藤代王子]]・[[切目王子]]・[[稲葉根王子]]・[[滝尻王子]]・[[発心門王子]]の5社とするのが一般的である。
=== 大辺路の王子社 === 冒頭に要約したような定義からは外れるが王子を名乗る神社が熊野にはある。[[大辺路]]にある幾つかの神社がそれである。大辺路の成立には[[当山派]]（[[真言宗]]系）修験が関係しているが、それらの神社の成立事情とは関係がない。また、成立時期も古くても[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝期]]以降のものであるほか、皇族の熊野参詣との関連もなく、九十九王子には含めない。大辺路の王子社については[[大辺路の王子社一覧]]を参照。
== 九十九王子の一覧 == [[ファイル:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|城南宮]] [[ファイル:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|八軒家船着場]] 九十九王子は、実際に99ちょうどあるわけではなく、多数あるということの比喩である<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">[[#toyoshima|豊島修 (1992)、52頁]]</ref>。九十九王子は、上述のような事情から列挙が一貫しない。最盛期の80余社にしても、いずれの神社を含めるかは参詣記ごとに様々である。それら参詣記の中で王子と認定された神社を、現存するもの、現存しないが所在地を比定できるものを併せて枚挙してゆくと、若干数の近世以降の比較的新しい王子を含めて、下記の様に101社を数える。ただし、近世における[[紀州藩]]による再建、[[明治時代|明治期]]の[[神社合祀令]]による合祀、市街化による廃絶など、いくつかの事情により、比定できるといってもその確度は様々である。なお、五体王子は'''ボールドで強調'''する。
なお、「九十九王子」の読み方には、「'''きゅうじゅうきゅうおうじ'''」（主に大阪地方）、「'''つくもおうじ'''」といった読み方もあるが、熊野地方で一般的なのは「'''くじゅうくおうじ'''」であり、本記事もそれに従っている。
* [[城南宮]]……[[京都]]からの出発点 * [[八軒家船着場]]……[[京都]]から[[大阪]]へ[[淀川]]を下ってきた船着場
1. [[窪津王子]] 2. [[坂口王子]] 3. [[郡戸王子]] 4. [[上野王子 (大阪市)|上野王子]] 5. [[安倍野王子|阿倍王子]] 6. [[津守王子]] : （以上、[[大阪市]]）
7. [[境王子]] 8. [[大鳥居王子]] : （以上、[[堺市]]）
9. [[篠田王子]] 10. [[平松王子]] 11. [[井ノ口王子]] : （以上、[[和泉市]]）
12. 池田王子 13. 麻生川王子 14. 近木王子 15. 鞍持王子 : （以上、[[岸和田市]]・[[貝塚市]]。[[九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]]を参照）
16. 鶴原王子 17. 佐野王子<!--[[佐野王子 (泉佐野市)]]--> 18. 籾井（樫井）王子 : （以上、[[泉佐野市]]。[[九十九王子 (泉佐野市)]]を参照）
19. 厩戸王子 20. 信達一ノ瀬王子 21. 長岡王子 : （以上、[[泉南市]]。[[九十九王子 (泉南市)]]を参照）
22. 地蔵堂王子 23. 馬目王子 : （以上、[[阪南市]]。[[九十九王子 (阪南市)]]を参照）
: ここまでが[[大阪府]]、次からが[[和歌山県]]。
24. 中山王子 25. 山口王子 26. 川辺王子 27. 中村王子 28. 吐前（吐崎）王子 29. 川端王子 30. 和佐王子 31. 平緒王子 32. 奈久知王子 : （以上、[[和歌山市]]。[[九十九王子 (和歌山市)]]参照。）
33. 松坂王子 34. 松代王子 35. 菩提房王子 36. 祓戸王子 37. '''[[藤代王子]]''' 38. 藤白塔下王子 39. 橘本王子 40. [[橘本神社|所坂王子]] 41. 一壷王子 : （以上、[[海南市]]。[[九十九王子 (海南市)]]参照。）
42. 蕪坂王子 43. 山口王子 44. 糸我王子 : （以上、[[有田市]]。[[九十九王子 (有田市)]]参照。）
45. 逆川王子 46. 湯浅（久米崎）王子 : （以上、[[湯浅町]]。[[九十九王子 (湯浅町)]]参照。）
47. 津兼（井関）王子 48. 河瀬王子 49. 馬留王子 : （以上、[[広川町 (和歌山県)|広川町]]。[[九十九王子 (広川町)]]参照。）

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[九十九王子]] to [[Kujūku Ōji]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''九十九王子'''（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた[[修験道|熊野修験]]の手で急速に組織された一群の神社のことをいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。したがって、その分布は[[紀伊路]]・[[中辺路]]の沿道に限られる。 [[ファイル:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|多富気王子跡（[[大門坂]]）]]
== 概要 == === 出現と盛衰 === 王子の名の初出史料と考えられているのは、[[増基]]の手による参詣記『いほぬし（庵主）』にある「わうじのいはや（王子の岩屋）」で、[[花窟神社|花の窟]]についての記事に登場する。この文書の成立年代は[[10世紀]]後半から[[11世紀]]半ばまでと見られているが、これに次ぐのは[[1081年|1082]]（[[永保|永保元]]）年の[[藤原為房]]の日記『[[大御記]]』の日根王子での[[奉幣]]の記事であり、少なくとも[[11世紀]]には熊野に王子と呼ばれる神社があったことが確認できる。[[12世紀]]に入ると王子社が急増乱立し、史料にも新王子の記述が増える。[[13世紀]]になってもしばらくはこの傾向が続き、12 - 13世紀に最盛期を迎える。だが、こうした新王子の中には、短命のものもかなりあった。その後、おおむね[[鎌倉時代]]以降に、熊野詣の主体の変化や熊野詣自体の後退に伴って、多くは衰退するにいたった。
=== 王子とは何か === 王子は参詣途上で儀礼を行う場所であった。主たる儀礼は奉幣と経供養（[[般若心経]]などを読経する）であり、神仏混淆的である。だが、よく言われるような[[熊野三山]]遥拝が行われた形跡は（少なくとも史料上では）確認できない。また、帰路にはほとんど顧みられることがないことから、物品の補給をおこなったとする説もあたらないと考えられている。これらの儀式が王子で行われたのは、王子とは[[熊野権現]]の御子神であるとの認識があり、すなわち参詣者の庇護が期待されたのである。
これら王子の形成は、中世熊野詣において先達をつとめた熊野の修験者によるものである。本来は沿道住人の祀る雑多な在地の神々である諸社を王子と認定したのは、熊野参詣を主導する先達たちである（ただし解釈は一貫せず、参詣記ごと・先達ごとのばらつきが相当ある）。また、'''王子'''という命名も、峯中修行者を守護する神仏は童子の姿をとるという[[修験道]]の思想に基づくものであると考えられる。同時に、熊野修験は院政期以降の皇族・貴人たちの参詣の先達をつとめたが、このことは、九十九王子の顕著な特徴である、紀伊路・中辺路への集中や院政期に重なる12世紀の大量出現をもたらしている。実際、最盛期に80余を数えた王子の大半は、皇族の参詣の活発化に伴って組織されたものである。
=== 五体王子 === [[ファイル:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|藤代王子]] [[ファイル:切目王子.jpg|thumb|切目王子]] [[ファイル:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|滝尻王子]] [[ファイル:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|発心門王子]] 九十九王子の中には五体王子（ごたいおうじ）と呼ばれる王子があり、他とは格式を異にするとされる。これらは一般に、熊野の主神の御子神ないし眷属神として三山に祀られる神々のなかでも、五所王子と呼ばれる神々（若一王子・禅師宮・聖宮・児宮・子守宮）を祀る神社であり、三山から勧請したものと考えられている。これら五体王子では、舞・[[白拍子]]・[[神楽]]・[[猿楽]]などが行われており、芸能的性格が強いと一応は言えるが、これらは他の王子でも行われており、顕著な特徴とは言えない。なお、五'''大'''王子と書くのは誤りである。
いずれの王子を五体王子に数えるかは解釈の相違があり、[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記では[[籾井（樫井）王子|籾井（樫井）]]・[[藤代王子|藤代]]・[[稲葉根王子|稲葉根]]の3社を挙げるが、[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽院]]参詣記は[[藤代王子]]のみが該当し、[[稲葉根王子]]がこれに準じるとしているが、他の列挙例もあって一貫しない。現在では、[[藤代王子]]・[[切目王子]]・[[稲葉根王子]]・[[滝尻王子]]・[[発心門王子]]の5社とするのが一般的である。
=== 大辺路の王子社 === 冒頭に要約したような定義からは外れるが王子を名乗る神社が熊野にはある。[[大辺路]]にある幾つかの神社がそれである。大辺路の成立には[[当山派]]（[[真言宗]]系）修験が関係しているが、それらの神社の成立事情とは関係がない。また、成立時期も古くても[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝期]]以降のものであるほか、皇族の熊野参詣との関連もなく、九十九王子には含めない。大辺路の王子社については[[大辺路の王子社一覧]]を参照。
== 九十九王子の一覧 == [[ファイル:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|城南宮]] [[ファイル:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|八軒家船着場]] 九十九王子は、実際に99ちょうどあるわけではなく、多数あるということの比喩である<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">[[#toyoshima|豊島修 (1992)、52頁]]</ref>。九十九王子は、上述のような事情から列挙が一貫しない。最盛期の80余社にしても、いずれの神社を含めるかは参詣記ごとに様々である。それら参詣記の中で王子と認定された神社を、現存するもの、現存しないが所在地を比定できるものを併せて枚挙してゆくと、若干数の近世以降の比較的新しい王子を含めて、下記の様に101社を数える。ただし、近世における[[紀州藩]]による再建、[[明治時代|明治期]]の[[神社合祀令]]による合祀、市街化による廃絶など、いくつかの事情により、比定できるといってもその確度は様々である。なお、五体王子は'''ボールドで強調'''する。
なお、「九十九王子」の読み方には、「'''きゅうじゅうきゅうおうじ'''」（主に大阪地方）、「'''つくもおうじ'''」といった読み方もあるが、熊野地方で一般的なのは「'''くじゅうくおうじ'''」であり、本記事もそれに従っている。

{{デフォルトソート:くしゆうくおうし}} [[Category:九十九王子|*]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:修験道の神]]

'''Kujuku Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|qq=draft}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|qq=draft}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano|ja|熊野|qq=draft}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|qq=draft}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|qq=draft}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|qq=draft}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|qq=draft}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|ja|花窟神社|qq=draft}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|qq=draft}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|qq=draft}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|qq=draft}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|qq=draft}} in {{ill|1082|永保元年}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura Period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Five Major Ōji|ja|五体王子|qq=draft}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Five Sons of Kumano|ja|五所王子|qq=draft}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮). The five major Ōji include {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|ja|藤代王子|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kirime Ōji|ja|切目王子|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inabane Ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|ja|滝尻王子|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|qq=draft}}.
=== Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|qq=draft}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Kujuku Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|qq=draft}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Nanboku-chō Period|ja|南北朝時代|qq=draft}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Kujuku Ōji.
== List of Kujuku Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Kujuku Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records.{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}} == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shugendō|ja|修験道|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|ja|熊野古道|qq=draft}}
{{Kujūku Ōji|bottom}}
{{translated page|ja|九十九王子|version=102494402|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}
