<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}; {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723}}; {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314}}}} '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135493040}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano region|en|Kumano region|WD=Q6443450|lt=Kumano}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihonushi (庵主)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 増基法師集|1=Ihonushi (庵主)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429161}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuki|1=Masumoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429162}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|en|Hananoiwaya Shrine|WD=Q11615617|lt=Hananoiwaya Shrine}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Goki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 為房卿記|1=Tamefusa-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567178}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tamefusa|1=Fujiwara no Tamefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623393}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445508}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hine Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1082|en|1082|WD=Q19452|lt=1082}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura Period}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Gotai Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Major Ōji|fr|Gotai-ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.
== List of Ninety-Nine Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Ninety-Nine Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>

This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Goki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 為房卿記|1=Tamefusa-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567178}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tamefusa|1=Fujiwara no Tamefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623393}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445508}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hine Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1082|en|1082|WD=Q19452|lt=1082}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura Period}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Gotai Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Major Ōji|fr|Gotai-ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.
== List of Ninety-Nine Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Ninety-Nine Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records. == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730}}; {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723}}; {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314}}}} '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135493040}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano region|en|Kumano region|WD=Q6443450|lt=Kumano}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihonushi (庵主)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 増基法師集|1=Ihonushi (庵主)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429161}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuki|1=Masumoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429162}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|en|Hananoiwaya Shrine|WD=Q11615617|lt=Hananoiwaya Shrine}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Goki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 為房卿記|1=Tamefusa-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567178}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tamefusa|1=Fujiwara no Tamefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623393}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445508}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hine Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1082|en|1082|WD=Q19452|lt=1082}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura Period}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Gotai Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Major Ōji|fr|Gotai-ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.
== List of Ninety-Nine Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Ninety-Nine Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records. == References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Hasegawa Yasutaka, ''Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook'', Shinpū Shobō, 2007. ISBN 9784882696292 * Koyama Yasunori, ''Kumano Kōdō'', Iwanami Shoten, 2000. ISBN 4004306655 ...

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}; {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}; {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}}} '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135493040|1=Kumano Shugendo}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano region|en|Kumano region|WD=Q6443450|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano region}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihonushi (庵主)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 増基法師集|1=Q11429161|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429161}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuki|1=Masumoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429162}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|en|Hananoiwaya Shrine|WD=Q11615617|lt=Hananoiwaya Shrine|1=Hananoiwaya Shrine}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Goki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 為房卿記|1=Tamefusa-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567178}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tamefusa|1=Fujiwara no Tamefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623393}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445508}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hine Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1082|en|1082|WD=Q19452|lt=1082|1=1082}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura Period|1=Kamakura period}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Gotai Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Major Ōji|fr|Gotai-ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori|1=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.
== List of Ninety-Nine Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Ninety-Nine Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records. == References == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Kujūkuōji]][[ja:九十九王子]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}; {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}; {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}}} '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135493040|1=Kumano Shugendo}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano region|en|Kumano region|WD=Q6443450|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano region}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihonushi (庵主)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 増基法師集|1=Q11429161|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429161}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuki|1=Masumoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429162}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|en|Hananoiwaya Shrine|WD=Q11615617|lt=Hananoiwaya Shrine|1=Hananoiwaya Shrine}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Goki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 為房卿記|1=Tamefusa-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567178}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tamefusa|1=Fujiwara no Tamefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623393}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445508}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hine Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1082|en|1082|WD=Q19452|lt=1082|1=1082}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura Period|1=Kamakura period}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Gotai Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Major Ōji|fr|Gotai-ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori|1=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.

=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]

These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori|1=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.
== List of Ninety-Nine Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Ninety-Nine Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records. == References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}; {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}; {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}}} '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' (九十九王子) refers to a group of shrines along the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} that were primarily organized between the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135493040|1=Kumano Shugendo}}, serving as guides for imperial and aristocratic pilgrimages to {{ill|Kumano region|en|Kumano region|WD=Q6443450|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano region}}. These shrines were prayed to for the protection of pilgrims and are distributed along the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} and {{ill|Nakahechi|ja|中辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahechi|1=Nakahechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30074932}} routes.
[[File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|180px|Tafuji Ōji site ({{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}})]]
== Overview ==
=== Emergence and Decline === The first documented mention of an Ōji is found in the pilgrimage record ''{{ill|Ihonushi (庵主)|ja|いほぬし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ihonushi (庵主)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 増基法師集|1=Q11429161|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429161}}'', attributed to {{ill|Masuki|ja|増基|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuki|1=Masumoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11429162}}, which describes the "Ōji Cave" (わうじのいはや) at the site of {{ill|Hananoiwaya Shrine|en|Hananoiwaya Shrine|WD=Q11615617|lt=Hananoiwaya Shrine|1=Hananoiwaya Shrine}}. This document is believed to have been written between the late 10th and mid-11th centuries. The next reference appears in {{ill|Dai Goki|ja|大御記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dai Goki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 為房卿記|1=Tamefusa-kyō-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567178}}, a diary by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tamefusa|ja|藤原為房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Tamefusa|1=Fujiwara no Tamefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623393}}, which records a {{ill|Hōbei|ja|奉幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōbei|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445508}} ritual at {{ill|Hine Ōji|ja|日根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hine Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1082|en|1082|WD=Q19452|lt=1082|1=1082}}. By the 12th century, the number of Ōji shrines increased rapidly, reaching their peak between the 12th and 13th centuries. However, many of these new Ōji were short-lived. After the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura Period|1=Kamakura period}}, changes in pilgrimage practices and a decline in Kumano pilgrimages led to their general decline.
=== What Are Ōji Shrines? === Ōji shrines were places where rituals were conducted during pilgrimages. Key rituals included hōbei and sutra chanting, often blending Buddhist and Shinto elements. Contrary to popular belief, no evidence suggests that these rituals included worshipping the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} from afar. The shrines were not used for resupplying goods, as their primary purpose was the protection of pilgrims. This belief stemmed from the recognition of Ōji as divine offspring of Kumano Gongen.
The organization of these shrines was carried out by Kumano Shugendō practitioners, who identified local deities along the pilgrimage routes and designated them as Ōji shrines. The term "Ōji" reflects the Shugendō belief that guardian deities often appear as childlike figures (童子).
=== Five Major Ōji Shrines === [[File:241006 Fujishiro-jinja Kainan Wakayama pref Japan30s3.jpg|thumb|Fujishiro Ōji]] [[File:切目王子.jpg|thumb|Kirime Ōji]] [[File:Takijiri-oji shrine 2.jpg|thumb|Takijiri Ōji]] [[File:HOSSHINMON-oji.JPG|thumb|Hosshinmon Ōji]]
Among the Ōji shrines, five are considered particularly prestigious, referred to as the {{ill|Gotai Ōji|ja|五体王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Major Ōji|fr|Gotai-ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. These shrines are thought to enshrine deities known as the {{ill|Gosho Ōji|ja|五所王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Sons of Kumano|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}} (若一王子, 禅師宮, 聖宮, 児宮, 子守宮).
* {{ill|Fujishiro Ōji|fr|Fujishiro-ōji|ja|藤白神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujishiro Ōji|Ōji=Ōji|Fujishiro-ōji=Fujishiro-ōji|1=Fujishiro Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3090605}} - {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|zh|饒速日命|en|Nigihayahi|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} * {{ill|Kirime Ōji|fr|Kirime-ōji|ja|切目王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirime Ōji|Kirime Ōji=Kirime Ōji|1=Kirime Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197362}} - {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu-Omikami|lt_ja=天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}} {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|en|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q1056017|lt=Ninigi|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} {{ill|Hoori|en|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hoori|1=Hoori}} {{ill|Ugayafukiaezu|en|Ugayafukiaezu|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|Inabane Ōji|fr|Inabane-ōji|ja|稲葉根王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabane Ōji|1=Inabane Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3149767}} - {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Ōkami|1=Inari Ōkami}} * {{ill|Takijiri Ōji|fr|Takijiri-ōji|ja|滝尻王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takijiri Ōji|1=Takijiri Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3514339}} * {{ill|Hosshinmon Ōji|ja|発心門王子|fr|Hosshinmon-ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosshinmon Ōji|1=Hosshinmon Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3141012}} === Ōji Shrines Along the Ōhechi === Several shrines along the {{ill|Ōhechi|ja|大辺路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhechi|1=Ōhechi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2748597}}, although bearing the name "Ōji," are not included in the Ninety-Nine Ōji definition. These shrines are associated with {{ill|Tōzan-ha|ja|当山派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōzan-ha|1=Tōzan-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487826}}, a school of Shugendō, and were established after the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō Period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}. They lack connections to imperial pilgrimages and are not considered part of the Ninety-Nine Ōji.
== List of Ninety-Nine Ōji == [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|thumb|Jōnangū Shrine]] [[File:八軒家船着場.JPG|thumb|Hakkenya Dock]]
The term "Ninety-Nine Ōji" does not indicate precisely 99 shrines but rather symbolizes a large number.<ref name="豊島修 (1992)、52頁">{{Cite book|和書|author=豊島修|title=死の国・熊野|publisher=講談社|series=講談社現代新書|year=1992|isbn=4-06-149103-2}}</ref>
Below is a list of 101 shrines, including those that have been reconstructed or identified through historical records. == References == {{Reflist}}
